ai.0: Area of Study 3 asks how fields make the electricity in your wall; the whole answer descends from one twitch of a meter needle in 1831. Read this in one sitting — Faraday's bench to the 500 kV lines leaving the Latrobe Valley — nothing solved, only followed.
ai.1: Some winter evening this year, Melbourne will switch itself on — kettles, trams, footy training under lights, and you, flicking a switch without a thought. Follow the wire behind that switch backwards: through the wall, under the street, through a suburban substation to a terminal station on the city's edge, then along the 500 kV lines that stride east across Gippsland. About 170 km from your kitchen, the wire ends at a machine in the Latrobe Valley.
ai.2: The machine is a coil of wire spinning in a magnetic field. That is the whole secret. In the Valley, steam raised by burning brown coal does the spinning; in the Snowy Mountains, falling water; on the windy plains west of Melbourne, moving air. Strip away the boilers and blades and almost every large generator on Earth is the same device: a coil of wire, turning between magnets.
ai.3: This Area of Study — its official title is How are fields used in electricity generation? — explains that one device and the network built around it. And it begins exactly where the last page left off: with the observation that a field can be static or changing, and that the changing kind is the kind that pays.
ai.4: Connects The last page flagged static versus changing as the consequential field classification. This Area of Study spends that idea on the grid; Unit 4 spends it again, on light itself.
ai.5: London, August 1831: Michael Faraday — the bookbinder's apprentice who gave us the field — had been circling one conviction for a decade. An electric current creates a magnetic field; Hans Christian Oersted had shown that in 1820. Surely, then, a magnetic field should create a current. Faraday wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a sensitive current meter — connected to the second, waiting for the steady current that symmetry seemed to promise.
ai.6: The needle gave him nothing. Or almost nothing: at the instant he connected the battery, it twitched, then fell back to zero and stayed there, however strong and steady the current in the first coil. At the instant he disconnected, it twitched again — the other way. The effect he had planned for was absent; the one he had not lived entirely at the edges, in the two brief moments when the magnetic field was changing.
ai.7: By October he had the clean version of the experiment. Push a bar magnet into a coil of wire and the needle kicks. Hold the magnet still inside the coil — for as long as you like, with as strong a magnet as you like — and nothing flows. Pull it out and the needle kicks the opposite way.
ai.8: Motion in, current one way; motion out, current the other; no motion, no current. The field does not drive charges through the wire. The change in the field does.
ai.9: The push that a changing field gives to charges is called an induced emf — short for electromotive force, a misleading old name: it is not a force, and it is measured in volts. The phenomenon as a whole is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page commits Faraday's accident deliberately, over and over. But "the field changes" is not precise enough to build a grid on. Exactly which quantity has to change?
ai.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with its full set of rules for what registers on the meter and what does not, opens Pass 2 at Faraday's law.
ai.11: Picture the wire loop as a window frame, and the magnetic field lines as threads passing through the opening. The quantity Faraday's discovery hinges on is the magnetic flux: how much field passes through the window. Hold the window square-on to the field and the flux is at its greatest; tilt it and less passes through; turn it edge-on and the lines slide past without entering at all — zero flux. The formula is named ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — field strength multiplied by the area held perpendicular to it — and flux earns its own unit, the weber (Wb).
ai.12: If induction needs changing flux, the formula is a list of handles. You can change B, by strengthening or weakening the field or moving the loop nearer the magnet or further away. You can change A, the area sitting inside the field, by sliding the loop in or out. Or you can change the angle, by rotating the loop so it shows the field its face, then its edge, then its face again — three handles: field, area, angle.
ai.13: Faraday's law says the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Read it as a sentence rather than solving it: more flux change in less time means a bigger push, and a coil of N turns is pushed N times over. The size of the flux is irrelevant; only its rate of change counts. The strongest field in the world, held steady, induces precisely nothing.
ai.14: Of the three handles, the grid grips the third. Spin a coil in a steady field and its flux swings from maximum, through zero, to maximum the other way, and back — change that never runs out. Hold that picture: it is the rest of this story, with one sunlit exception at the end.
ai.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change them are made precise in Magnetic flux — calculation for the perpendicular case, qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
ai.16: Faraday's law arrives wearing a minus sign, and the minus sign has its own name: Lenz's law, after Heinrich Lenz, who stated it in 1834. The induced current flows in the direction that opposes the change creating it. Push a magnet's north pole toward a coil and the induced current circulates so that the coil presents a north pole back, resisting the approach; pull the magnet away and the current reverses, now pulling the magnet back. Note the precise wording — the current opposes the change in flux, never the field itself.
ai.17: To see why it must oppose, run the alternative. If the induced current aided the change, a nudged magnet would be drawn in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a larger current, pulling harder still — a runaway spiral handing out energy for free, started by a fingertip. Energy conservation forbids it, and that is all Lenz's law is: energy conservation wearing a disguise. Every joule of electrical energy you induce must be paid for as work done against the opposition.
ai.18: This is why a generator resists being turned. Spin one connected to nothing and it turns freely; connect a lamp and the crank stiffens — you can feel, in your wrist, the energy the lamp is spending. Connect a suburb and it takes a power station's worth of steam to keep the coil turning. A braking electric car runs the bargain in reverse: its motor acts as a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force — banking energy in the battery instead of burning it in the brake pads.
ai.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz's law. The ledger they protect is the same energy bookkeeping you met in Position is stored energy.
ai.20: Now revisit a machine you already own. Area of Study 2 built the DC motor: a coil on an axle between magnet poles; current in, the two sides of the coil pushed in opposite directions, rotation out — with a split-ring commutator reversing the current every half-turn so that the rotation never changes direction.
ai.21: Unhook the battery and spin the coil yourself. Rotation changes the flux through the coil; changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out. The same hardware is now a generator — the film of the motor run backwards: a motor spends electrical energy to make motion; a generator spends motion to make electrical energy. Every label on the motor diagram survives unchanged; only the energy flow turns around.
ai.22: One design choice remains — how the spinning coil hands its current to the stationary outside world — and it decides everything about the output. Slip rings, two complete rings with one joined to each end of the coil, keep continuous contact and deliver the emf exactly as it is made: reversing direction every half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and the machine is called an alternator. The motor's own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the opposite: by swapping the connections every half-turn it cancels each reversal as it happens, so the outside current always flows one way — direct current, DC — rising and falling in bumps, but never reversing — one small fitting, two different products.
ai.23: Simulation 1.1 puts the crank in your hand.
ai.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror is at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is examined almost every year.
ai.25: Spin the coil at a perfectly steady rate and the induced emf traces the smoothest curve in physics: a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once per turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia's grid is tuned so that the swing completes 50 cycles every second — mains electricity at 50 Hz. Its size is quoted as 230 V, but that number hides a subtlety: the voltage at your wall actually swings between about +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That honest average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 unpacks the name.
ai.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and that wall, though, stands a physics problem. Wire has resistance, and current flowing through resistance turns precious electrical energy into useless heat — a loss named Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. Notice what is squared: the current.
ai.27: Power is voltage multiplied by current, so the same power can travel as a low voltage with a large current, or as an enormous voltage with a tiny current. Raise the voltage ten times and the current falls to a tenth — and the heating loss to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage is not a preference; it is the entire game.
ai.28: The machine that makes voltage adjustable is Faraday's iron ring grown up: the transformer. Two coils share an iron core; nothing moves. Alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core carries it through the secondary coil; Faraday's law induces an emf there, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up and the current steps down by the same factor, the power ideally untouched.
ai.29: Here, too, is why the grid runs on AC at all: a transformer works only with changing flux. Give it steady DC and the flux freezes — nothing is induced, and nothing comes out.
ai.30: So the journey in Figure 1.1 is Faraday's law applied over and over. A coil in the Valley induces about 20 kV; a step-up transformer lifts it to 500 kV for the long march to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down in stages; and a final transformer near your house delivers 230 V to the wall, 50 times a second, ready for the flick of a switch.
ai.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic are built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the exam's hardest regular question — waits in Transmission of electricity.
ai.32: One generator on the grid breaks the spinning-coil template entirely. More than four million Australian rooftops now carry photovoltaic cells — panels that sit silent and motionless in the sun while current flows out of them. No coil, no magnet, no rotation: nothing moves but electrons and light.
ai.33: Inside each cell is a thin sandwich of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct slightly differently. Arriving light knocks electrons loose, and an electric field, built permanently into the junction between the two slices, lets the loosened electrons cross in one direction only. A one-way push makes a one-way current: photovoltaic cells produce DC. A field is still doing all the work — the electric field this time, standing at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
ai.34: Your house and the grid speak AC, so every rooftop system includes one more box: the inverter, which converts the panels' DC into 230 V AC. The study design asks only what an inverter does, not how — its electronic circuitry, like the fine detail of the silicon sandwich, is officially beyond the course.
ai.35: One question is left deliberately hanging: why should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all? The answer needs a new idea about what light is — that it arrives in discrete packets of energy — and it is the opening move of Unit 4, where the packets get their name: photons.
ai.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4's photoelectric effect.
ai.37: You have now read Area of Study 3 once, end to end. Pass 2 retells it as core, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each meant for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday's law, Lenz's law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the rotating coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).
ai.38: Pass 3 is for exam season: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with the subtleties, the assessors' advice and exam-style questions worked in full. The Area of Study has been worth a steady 22 of the exam's 120 marks on every recent paper, and its favourite questions are loyal; you will meet them all there.
ai.39: Connects Begin Pass 2 · Electromagnetic induction whenever you are ready, and bookmark Pass 3 · Generation of electricity for when the exam is real.

socratic.0:
Socrates. When you flick a switch tonight and a lamp lights, where would you say that electricity is born?
Student. Somewhere far off, I suppose — a power station.
Socrates. And the whole of this enquiry — how fields make the electricity in your walls — descends from one small event: a single twitch of a meter needle in 1831. Shall we follow the wire from Faraday's bench all the way to the 500 kV lines leaving the Latrobe Valley? We shall solve nothing yet; we shall only trace it.
socratic.1:
Student. Then let us trace it backwards. It is a winter evening, Melbourne is drawing its load — kettles, trams, lighting, every domestic switch. Behind my own switch, where does the wire go?
Socrates. Follow it through the wall, beneath the street, into a suburban substation, on to a terminal station at the city's edge, then out along the 500 kV lines running east across Gippsland. How far do you think you have travelled before the wire ends?
Student. Surely many kilometres.
Socrates. About 170 of them — from your kitchen to a single machine in the Latrobe Valley.
socratic.2:
Student. What is this machine that sits at the far end?
Socrates. A coil of wire, turning in a magnetic field. Now, in the Latrobe Valley what makes it turn?
Student. Steam, I'd guess, raised by burning brown coal.
Socrates. Just so. And in the Snowy Mountains it is falling water; on the plains west of Melbourne, moving air. Yet set the boilers and the blades aside — what is left in every large generator?
Student. The same thing each time: a coil of wire turning between magnets.
socratic.3:
Socrates. That is what this Area of Study, How are fields used in electricity generation?, sets out to explain — that device, and the whole network built around it. Do you recall where the previous page left us?
Student. With the idea that a field can be either static or changing.
Socrates. And which of the two, do you suppose, makes electricity?
Student. If the whole story begins here, it must be the changing kind.
socratic.4:
Connects Socrates. So the distinction the last page drew — static versus changing — was the one that mattered?
Student. Yes; here we apply it to the grid, and I gather Unit 4 will apply it once more — to light itself.
socratic.5:
Socrates. Let us go back to the bench. London, August 1831. Michael Faraday — once a bookbinder's apprentice, and the man who first conceived the field — had clung to one conviction for ten years. Oersted had shown in 1820 that an electric current creates a magnetic field. What would you expect Faraday to suspect by symmetry?
Student. That a magnetic field, in turn, ought to create a current.
Socrates. So he wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a sensitive current meter — on the second. What was he expecting it to show?
Student. A steady current in the second coil, mirroring the first.
socratic.6:
Socrates. And the needle?
Student. Tell me — did it swing across?
Socrates. Almost nothing. At the instant the battery was connected, it deflected, then fell back to zero and stayed there — however strong and steady the current in the first coil. At the instant of disconnection, it deflected again, the opposite way. What does that pattern tell you about when the meter responds?
Student. The effect he predicted never came. The needle only moved in those two brief moments — and those were exactly the moments when the magnetic field was changing.
socratic.7:
Socrates. By October he had pared the experiment to its plainest form. Move a bar magnet into a coil of wire — what happens?
Student. The needle deflects.
Socrates. Hold the magnet still inside the coil, for as long as you like, with the strongest magnet you can find?
Student. Then I would expect nothing at all — and you are telling me there is nothing. No current.
Socrates. And withdraw it again?
Student. The needle deflects the other way.
socratic.8:
Socrates. Gather the three results, then. Inward?
Student. Current one way.
Socrates. Outward?
Student. Current the other way.
Socrates. And no motion?
Student. No current. So it is not the field itself that drives charge through the wire — it is the change in the field.
socratic.9:
Socrates. Good. The action a changing field exerts on charges is called an induced emf — short for electromotive force. But beware the name: is it a force?
Student. You're warning me it isn't. And it's measured in volts, not newtons.
Socrates. A misleading historical label, exactly. The whole phenomenon is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page reproduces Faraday's effect on purpose, over and over. But to say merely that "the field changes" — is that precise enough to build a grid upon?
Student. No. I'd want to know which quantity, precisely, must change.
socratic.10:
Connects Socrates. That full set of rules — magnet and coil, what registers on the meter and what does not — is where Pass 2 begins, isn't it?
Student. Yes, with Faraday's law.
socratic.11:
Socrates. So let us name the quantity. Picture the wire loop as a window frame, and the field lines as threads passing through the opening. How much field gets through — what shall we call that?
Student. Some measure of how much field passes through the loop.
Socrates. We call it the magnetic flux. When is the most field passing through — loop square-on to the field, tilted, or edge-on?
Student. Square-on, surely. Tilt it and less passes; turn it edge-on and the lines slip past without entering — zero flux.
Socrates. Just so. We write it ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — the field strength times the area held perpendicular to it. And flux earns its own unit, the weber (Wb).
socratic.12:
Socrates. Now, since induction needs the flux to change, that little formula is really a list of what we may change. Look at B — how could you alter it?
Student. Strengthen or weaken the field, or move the loop nearer or further from the magnet.
Socrates. And A, the area within the field?
Student. Slide the loop in or out, so more or less of it sits in the field.
Socrates. And the angle?
Student. Rotate the loop — facing the field, then edge-on, then facing again. So three handles in all: field, area, and angle.
socratic.13:
Socrates. Faraday's law now puts a number on it: the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, times the number of turns — ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Put it in words for me.
Student. More flux change in less time means a bigger emf — and a coil of N turns is driven N times over.
Socrates. So does it matter how large the flux is?
Student. No — only how fast it changes. The strongest field, held perfectly steady, induces nothing at all.
socratic.14:
Socrates. Of our three handles, which do you think the grid reaches for?
Student. Given that machine — a coil turning between magnets — it must be the third: the angle.
Socrates. And when a coil rotates in a steady field, what does its flux do?
Student. It swings — maximum, through zero, to maximum the other way, and back. The change never stops.
Socrates. That rotating coil carries the rest of our story — save for one exception we'll meet at the very end.
socratic.15:
Connects Socrates. And where is all this — flux, the weber, the three ways to change them — made exact?
Student. In Magnetic flux — the calculation for the perpendicular case, and qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
socratic.16:
Socrates. You'll have noticed Faraday's law carries a minus sign. It has a name of its own — Lenz's law, stated by Heinrich Lenz in 1834. It says the induced current flows in the direction that opposes the change that creates it. Push a magnet's north pole toward a coil — what does the coil do in return?
Student. If it opposes the change, the coil must present a north pole back, resisting the approach.
Socrates. And withdraw it?
Student. The current reverses and opposes the withdrawal instead. So the current opposes the change in flux — not the field itself.
socratic.17:
Socrates. Why must it oppose, though? Suppose it didn't — suppose the induced current aided the change. Follow that through.
Student. Then a nudged magnet would be drawn in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a larger current, pulling harder still — it would run away on its own.
Socrates. Producing what, from what?
Student. Energy out of a single small push — energy from nothing. Conservation forbids it.
Socrates. So Lenz's law is, at bottom, what?
Student. A consequence of energy conservation. Every joule induced must be paid for — as work done against the opposition.
socratic.18:
Socrates. Then tell me why a generator resists being turned.
Student. Connected to nothing, it spins freely. Connect a lamp and it grows harder to turn — that resistance is the energy the lamp draws.
Socrates. And a whole suburb?
Student. A power station's steam is needed just to keep the coil turning.
Socrates. Now reverse the picture — an electric car braking.
Student. Its motor runs as a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force itself — storing the energy in the battery instead of throwing it away as heat in the pads.
socratic.19:
Connects Socrates. The direction rules — and the three-step recipe for getting them right?
Student. They live at Lenz's law — and the ledger they protect is the same energy bookkeeping from Position is stored energy.
socratic.20:
Socrates. Let us reconsider a machine you already met. Area of Study 2 gave you the DC motor — recall its parts.
Student. A coil on an axle between magnet poles. Feed current in, and the two sides of the coil are pushed opposite ways, so it rotates.
Socrates. And what keeps it turning the same way?
Student. A split-ring commutator, reversing the current every half-turn so the rotation never doubles back.
socratic.21:
Socrates. Now disconnect the battery and instead turn the coil by hand. Walk it through.
Student. Turning it changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out.
Socrates. So what has the motor become?
Student. A generator — the motor run backwards. A motor spends electrical energy to make motion; a generator spends motion to make electrical energy.
Socrates. And how much of the diagram do we redraw?
Student. Not a single label — only the direction of energy flow is reversed.
socratic.22:
Socrates. One design choice is left: how does the spinning coil hand its current to the still, outside circuit? And that choice fixes the shape of the output. Try slip rings — two full rings, each tied to one end of the coil. What do they deliver?
Student. They stay in contact all the way round, so they give the emf just as it's made — reversing every half-turn. That's alternating current, AC. The machine would be an alternator.
Socrates. And the motor's own fitting, the split-ring commutator?
Student. It does the opposite — swapping the connections each half-turn, cancelling every reversal as it happens. So the outside current always flows one way: direct current, DC, rising and falling but never reversing.
Socrates. One spinning coil, then — and from one fitting or the other?
Student. Two entirely different outputs.
socratic.23:
Socrates. Take the crank yourself in Simulation 1.1 and turn it — watch which fitting gives which.
Student. I will.
socratic.24:
Connects Socrates. Where is each half of this mirror set out in full?
Student. The motor half at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is examined almost every year.
socratic.25:
Socrates. When the coil turns at a perfectly steady rate, what shape does the emf trace out?
Student. A sine wave — swinging positive, then negative, once per turn as the flux rises and falls.
Socrates. Australia's grid completes how many of those swings each second?
Student. Fifty — mains electricity at 50 Hz.
Socrates. We quote its size as 230 V. But the voltage at the wall actually swings between about +325 V and −325 V. How can both be true?
Student. Then the 230 V isn't a peak — it must be the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power.
Socrates. That equivalent average has a name: the root-mean-square, or rms, value. Pass 2 will explain where the name comes from.
socratic.26:
Socrates. Between the Latrobe Valley and your wall, though, a difficulty waits. Wire has resistance — and current through resistance does what?
Student. Turns electrical energy into heat. A loss.
Socrates. Given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. Which quantity in there is squared?
Student. The current. So the current is what really punishes us.
socratic.27:
Socrates. Now, power is voltage times current. If I must deliver a fixed power, how might I send it?
Student. Either as low voltage with large current, or high voltage with small current.
Socrates. Raise the voltage tenfold — what becomes of the current, and of the heating loss?
Student. The current drops to a tenth, and since the loss goes as current squared, the heating falls to a hundredth.
Socrates. Over 170 km of line, then, is high voltage a luxury?
Student. No — a necessity.
socratic.28:
Socrates. What device lets us set the voltage where we please? It is Faraday's iron ring, grown up — the transformer. Two coils share an iron core, and nothing moves. Send alternating current into the primary — then what?
Student. It makes a changing flux; the core carries that flux through the secondary; and by Faraday's law an emf is induced there.
Socrates. Stepped up or down by what rule?
Student. In exact proportion to the turns on each coil — N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet.
Socrates. And when the voltage steps up?
Student. The current steps down by the same factor — the power, ideally, unchanged.
socratic.29:
Socrates. Here is a clue to a larger question. A transformer works only with changing flux. What would happen if we fed it steady DC?
Student. The flux would hold constant, nothing would be induced, and nothing would come out the other side.
Socrates. So why does the whole grid run on AC?
Student. Because without changing flux the transformers would be dead — and we couldn't step the voltage up and down at all.
socratic.30:
Socrates. So the journey in Figure 1.1 — what is it, really, repeated over and over?
Student. Faraday's law, applied again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces around 20 kV; a step-up transformer lifts it to 500 kV for the run to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down in stages; and a last transformer near the house brings it to 230 V at the wall — fifty times a second.
socratic.31:
Connects Socrates. And where is the arithmetic built properly — the rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss sums?
Student. In Transformers and transmission — and the full supply-system calculation, the exam's hardest regular question, waits in Transmission of electricity.
socratic.32:
Socrates. I promised one exception to the rotating coil. More than four million Australian rooftops now carry photovoltaic cells. What is curious about how they sit there?
Student. They don't move at all — they just lie still in the sun while current flows out of them.
Socrates. No coil, no magnet, no rotation. So what moves?
Student. Only electrons and light.
socratic.33:
Socrates. Look inside a cell. There is a thin layer of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct a little differently. Light falls in — what does it do?
Student. It displaces electrons.
Socrates. And built permanently into the junction between the two slices is an electric field, which lets those displaced electrons cross one way only. What kind of current must that give?
Student. A one-way action makes a one-way current — so photovoltaic cells produce DC.
Socrates. And what does the work here — is a field still at the heart of it?
Student. Yes — an electric field this time, sitting at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
socratic.34:
Socrates. But your house and the grid run on AC, while the panels make DC. What must every rooftop system add?
Student. One more piece — an inverter, to turn the panels' DC into 230 V AC.
Socrates. And how deeply need we understand it?
Student. Only what it does, not how. The electronic circuitry — like the fine detail of the silicon — sits beyond the course.
socratic.35:
Socrates. One question lingers, though. Why should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all?
Student. I can't answer that from anything we've said.
Socrates. No — it needs a new idea about the very nature of light: that it arrives in discrete packets of energy. That is the opening of Unit 4, where those packets get their name.
Student. Photons.
socratic.36:
Connects Socrates. Where are the cell and its inverter laid out, and where the reason light can eject electrons?
Student. The cell and inverter in Photovoltaic cells; the reason, in Unit 4's photoelectric effect.
socratic.37:
Socrates. We have now read Area of Study 3 once, in full. What does Pass 2 do with it?
Student. Retells it as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each meant for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction — flux, Faraday's law, Lenz's law and their graphs; Generators and alternators — the rotating coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics; and Transformers and transmission — rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic.
socratic.38:
Socrates. And Pass 3 — what is it for?
Student. Exam preparation: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with the subtleties, the assessors' advice and exam-style questions worked in full.
Socrates. Is it worth the labour?
Student. A steady 22 of the exam's 120 marks on every recent paper, with consistent recurring question types — and all of them appear there.
socratic.39:
Connects Socrates. So where do we go next, and where does the exam preparation live?
Student. Pass 2 · Electromagnetic induction is the next chapter; and Pass 3 · Generation of electricity holds the exam preparation for this material.
